dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for chest pain.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
Uh, chest pain and dizziness, started about 30 minutes ago.
Can you tell me more about the chest pain and if there's anything that makes it better or worse?
Are you known to have any medical conditions or surgeries in the past?
I have a medical history that includes an artificial hip joint.
How are you feeling right now? Can you hear me?
Yes, I can hear you.
What is your name?
My name is James.
Can you squeeze my hand?
Yes, I can do that.
You are awake and responding. Can you tell me the date today?
It's April 2nd, 2019.
Your airway is patent. Do you have any difficulty breathing or throat tightness?
No, I don't have either.
Let me listen to your breath sounds and observe your effort.
Your respiration rate is 18 breaths per minute, with a normal depth and no signs of cyanosis or use of accessory muscles.
Let me assess your circulation. Your pulse is 61, strong, and regular.
No signs of pale skin, mottling, or excessive moisture on peripheral limbs.
We are now taking your blood pressure.
Your pulse remains at 61 beats per minute.
Your respirations are still 18 breaths per minute.
Blood pressure is currently 187 over 85.
Your oxygen saturation is 97 percent on room air.
Blood glucose level is 131 mg/dL at this time.
We will now attach the cardiac monitor for EKG assessment.
I will check your skin for temperature, turgor, and any signs of abnormalities. It appears warm and dry with no visible abnormalities.
I am visually inspecting your head and neck area. No signs of abnormal neck veins or rigidity.
I will assess your nose and mouth, but found no signs of obstruction or drainage at this time.
No adventitious lung sounds and breath sounds are equal bilaterally.
I am administering aspirin 324 mg by mouth for possible heart-related symptoms.
We are now placing you on a cardiac monitor.
A 12-lead EKG is now being obtained for accurate cardiac assessment.
I am placing an IV in your left arm for potential fluid or medication administration.
The EKG is currently showing signs of a STEMI in the inferior wall with posterior extension.
We are preparing to transport you to Chippenham Hospital with rapid service.
Using a Reeves stretcher, we'll assist in bringing you into the ambulance safely.
Transfer to the main stretcher and securing seat belts for transport safety.
The EKG now shows a sinus bradycardia consistent with the previous findings.
We will be using lights and sirens for expedited transport.
Patient will be handed off to hospital nursing staff upon arrival.
Another EKG was completed and shows persistent STEMI findings.
Taking your vitals again now.
Blood pressure remains at 161 over 78 with pulse at 60.
Oxygen saturation is 98 and blood glucose is temporarily reading at zero - we'll repeat it soon.
With five points of safety, we're readying for transport.
How's your chest pain now, on a 10-point scale?
It's still a 4 with no new changes or worsening symptoms.
Secured onto the stretcher with the use of five seat belts for transport.
Let me double-check your history. You have an artificial hip and no known drug allergies?
That's correct. No drug allergies, and the hip is my only known surgery.
No other conditions or medications that I have missed from the initial history?
Just a current medication with Lisinopril.
We are now en route to the hospital with lights and sirens as planned.
All steps are following the STEMI transport protocol for rapid and safe delivery.
Repeating the EKG showed no significant new cardiac abnormalities.
You are being transported from the woods using a Reeves stretcher and main stretcher system.
No ambulation due to dizziness and inability to stand or walk safely.
The patient remains fully oriented throughout transport.
We are secured at this time and ready for hospital arrival.
The patient is now in the emergency room and ready for handover.
Stretcher is being wheeled into the ER for staff nurse handover.
Patient safely transferred into a hospital bed by staff.
Handover to staff RN顺利完成 without exception, as noted in ePCR.
All given medications including aspirin and IV placement are documented.
Continuous cardiac monitoring was ongoing during transport.
Transport was completed following all STEMI protocol steps.
No incidents or complications were encountered during the entire call.
The patient will now remain under hospital care for further assessment.
We completed our duties and are exiting the scene now per protocol.
Final vitals on record before transport handover included a normoxic and stable airway.
The STEMI alert is maintained to Chippenham for active management.
Final EKG is in electronic record and sending with patient handover.
Call summary and all interventions are now on electronic documentation.
Is there still dizziness or any new symptoms during transport?
Dizziness is still present and chest pain remains a 4.
No signs of instability and all vitals remain constant.
Patient remains alert and oriented for the final checks before hospital arrival.
Chippenham will process with emergency catheterization due to STEMI findings.
No further field interventions required per hospital and protocol.
Third EKG repeat confirms STEMI inferior wall with no rhythm changes.
All steps consistent with rapid safe transport and protocol compliance.
No delay in notes or documentation, and all steps followed the standard of care.
We have now reached the hospital, and preparations for transfer begin.
All necessary records and equipment are with the patient for organized transfer.
Return of EKG findings with partner noting no progression or regression.
Aspirin and IV are still intact and properly noted for hospital staff during check.
We have secured and provided care consistent with protocol.
All minor care events, including initial movement and EKG steps, are on record.
Any new information or changes from initial report?
No, just the same dizziness and moderate chest pain as before.
All is ready for transfer to hospital staff with no further field actions by us.
No further later questions or concerns believed in transport phase.
I will now assist in transferring the stretcher to the hospital ER.
First steps of hospital handover now ongoing.
All equipment, medications, and notes in place for this transfer.
No handover delays and consistent fast transport for high acuity patient.
All actions and interventions as per STEMI protocol for Hot Patient.
The patient now under ED management and we are clearing the scene.
All vitals consistent and within the tolerable range for safety.
No additional interventions required at this time.
Call concluded successfully with no delays and no complications observed.
All field notes and exams are complete, ready for hospital handover.
Transportation team has no further field tasks at this point.
Patient care summary turned over per standard.
EMS units have turned back to standby and respecting protocol.
Final pain assessment remains consistent, no spikes or sudden drops.
No additional chest pain, dizziness, or other symptoms reported until end.
No incidents, errors, or unsafe acts during the entire call.
The call was product of emergency stroke protocol and thorough prehospital care.
Documented per hospital protocol: STEMI, CT Chest, and catheterization considered.
All patient and staff were safe throughout transport and it was a well-managed call.
All porters and nursing staff received all correct reports and timely care.
Chippenham is advised and ready for the incoming STEMI case with all records.
Final transfer of care as per hospital protocol and bill of rights.
Handover happens in ER, and EMS is dismissed from scene.
No issues reported and no further documentation or interventions needed from EMS.
Time recorded, call concluded, and record transmitted to dispatch.
Unit available for emergency or non-emergency calls after this call.
